### Post by charonn0 on 2009-05-30

Here's my fstab file:

### Post by DeMus on 2009-05-30
> **charonn0 said:**
> No matter what I do, I can't seem to get my NTFS volumes to mount. I think that I'm not getting the right UUID's but I'm not sure. The volumes all mount if I click on them in Nautilus, but they won't automount.

Here's my fstab file:

#

Here's the result of blkid:



And here's the output of the mount command with all volumes mounted:



I just did a clean install of Ubuntu 9.04, all updates installed.

Reboot and do this:

Auto mount Windows disks
Fire up a terminal, to do this click Applications > Accessories > Terminal
Then type (or copy/paste) the following - 1 line at a time
```

sudo aptitude update
sudo aptitude install ntfs-config
```
Ok so when that returns you to user@pcname, that should be installed                 

Next, make sure you have NO drives mounted (they'll usually appear on your desktop). If you have disks mounted, right-click the icons (one by one) and choose unmount.
They also appear when opening Nautilus.
Then run the program from System > Administration > NTFS Configuration Tool

Enter your password when prompted - and choose the drives that you want to be automounted. Click Apply.

Now simply make sure that "Enable Write Support for Internal Drives" is checked and click OK.

---

### Post by charonn0 on 2009-05-30
I had already installed ntfs-config. When run, I get a window with two options: "Enable Write Support For Internal Device" and "Enable Write Support For External Device." Neither of these options, when enabled in any combination cause my drives to automount.

Any other ideas?

---

### Post by DeMus on 2009-05-30
> **charonn0 said:**
> I had already installed ntfs-config. When run, I get a window with two options: "Enable Write Support For Internal Device" and "Enable Write Support For External Device." Neither of these options, when enabled in any combination cause my drives to automount.

Any other ideas?

This would mean your drives are already mounted. Try unmounting them before starting ntfs-config and see what happens then.

---

### Post by charonn0 on 2009-05-30
Ok, made double sure that the volumes were not mounted. I ran sudo ntfs-config and got this screen:

[IMG]http://i51.photobucket.com/albums/f351/charonn0/Screenshot-2.png[/IMG]

It looks like what I need, so I checked all the boxes and clicked "Apply."

I get this error message in turn for each volume:

### Post by charonn0 on 2009-06-01
I figured it out! Silly me, In the fstab file, I was putting uuid instead of UUID.

---

i had successfully enabled automount in my pc.thx for the forum reply:KS
